quiz_questions.question_text: What is net force?

options.text: The combination of all forces acting on an object
The largest single force acting on an object
The mass of the object times its total volume
The friction between two surfaces in contact

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force is the vector sum of all forces acting on an object. Forces in the same direction add together, while forces in opposite directions subtract. The net force determines the object's acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: If two forces act on an object in the same direction, how do you find the net force?

options.text: Subtract the smaller from the larger force
Add the two forces together
Multiply the two forces together
Divide one force by the other one

quiz_questions.explanation: When two forces act in the same direction, they reinforce each other. You add their magnitudes to get the net force. For example, 5 N right + 3 N right = 8 N right.

quiz_questions.question_text: If two forces act in opposite directions, how do you find the net force?

options.text: Add their magnitudes together for the total
Multiply their magnitudes together for total
Subtract the smaller from the larger force
Divide the larger by the smaller force value

quiz_questions.explanation: When forces act in opposite directions, they partially cancel. Subtract the smaller from the larger to get the net force. The direction of the net force is the same as the larger force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the net force when a 10 N force pushes right and a 10 N force pushes left on the same object?

options.text: 20 N to the right direction
20 N to the left direction
10 N to the right direction
0 N, the forces are balanced

quiz_questions.explanation: Equal forces in opposite directions cancel out: 10 N - 10 N = 0 N. The net force is zero, which means the forces are balanced and the object will not accelerate.

quiz_questions.question_text: According to Newton's second law, what does net force equal?

options.text: Mass times acceleration (F = ma)
Mass times velocity of the object
Weight times the height of object
Volume times the density of object

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's second law states that net force equals mass times acceleration (F = ma). A larger net force produces a larger acceleration, and a larger mass requires more force for the same acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to an object when the net force on it is zero?

options.text: It accelerates in a random direction
It immediately comes to a full stop
It stays at rest or moves at constant velocity
It begins spinning in a circular motion

quiz_questions.explanation: When the net force is zero, the object is in equilibrium. It either stays at rest or continues moving at constant velocity. This follows from Newton's first law (the law of inertia).

quiz_questions.question_text: A 20 N force pushes an object right and a 5 N friction force acts left. What is the net force?

options.text: 25 N to the right direction
15 N to the right direction
15 N to the left direction
5 N to the right direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force = 20 N right - 5 N left = 15 N to the right. The push is larger than friction, so the object accelerates to the right with a net force of 15 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: What unit is force measured in?

options.text: Kilograms for the force
Meters per second squared
Newtons for the force
Joules for the force

quiz_questions.explanation: Force is measured in newtons (N), named after Sir Isaac Newton. One newton is the force needed to accelerate a 1 kg mass at 1 m/s squared.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the net force on an object is not zero, what must the object be doing?

options.text: Accelerating in the direction of the net force
Moving at a perfectly constant velocity here
Remaining completely at rest and stationary
Getting heavier due to the unbalanced forces

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-zero net force always causes acceleration in the direction of the net force (Newton's second law). The object may speed up, slow down, or change direction depending on the net force direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Three people push a stalled car: 100 N, 150 N, and 200 N all in the same direction. What is the net force?

options.text: 200 N because only the largest counts
150 N because it is the average value
450 N in the direction of the push
100 N because only the smallest counts

quiz_questions.explanation: Forces in the same direction simply add together: 100 + 150 + 200 = 450 N. All three pushes combine to create a net force of 450 N in the direction they are all pushing.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is net force?

options.text: The single force that has the same effect as all forces combined
The force produced by an object's mass and volume together
The total energy an object possesses at any given moment
The speed at which an object is currently moving along a path

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force is the single equivalent force that produces the same effect as all individual forces acting on an object combined. It determines whether and how the object accelerates.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'balanced forces' mean?

options.text: Forces that cause the object to accelerate quickly
Forces that are not acting on the object at all
Forces that cancel out giving a net force of zero
Forces that all point in the same one direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Balanced forces are forces that cancel each other out, resulting in a net force of zero. When forces are balanced, the object does not accelerate; it stays at rest or moves at constant velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'unbalanced forces' mean?

options.text: Forces that result in zero net force on the object
Forces that result in a non-zero net force
Forces that do not exist in the real world at all
Forces that are equal in size and direction always

quiz_questions.explanation: Unbalanced forces do not cancel out, resulting in a non-zero net force. This net force causes the object to accelerate, changing its velocity in the direction of the net force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'Newton's second law' mean?

options.text: Net force equals mass times acceleration (F = ma)
An object at rest stays at rest without a push
For every action there is an equal reaction
Energy cannot be created or destroyed at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's second law states that the acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force and inversely proportional to its mass. The formula is F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'vector' mean in the context of forces?

options.text: A quantity with both magnitude and direction
A quantity with only magnitude, no direction
A unit of measurement for energy values
The mass of an object at rest on a surface

quiz_questions.explanation: A vector has both magnitude (size) and direction. Force is a vector quantity because it has both a size (measured in newtons) and a direction (like left, right, up, or down).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'magnitude' mean when describing a force?

options.text: The direction in which the force is acting
The size or strength of the force in newtons
The type of force such as gravity or friction
The object on which the force is acting upon

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnitude is the size or strength of a force, expressed as a number with units (newtons). A 50 N force has a greater magnitude than a 20 N force. Direction is described separately.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'resultant force' mean?

options.text: Another term for the net force on an object
The force that an object exerts on others
The force that remains after motion stops
The weakest force acting on the object here

quiz_questions.explanation: Resultant force is another term for net force. It is the single force that results from combining all forces acting on an object. It has both a magnitude and a direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'acceleration' mean in relation to net force?

options.text: The rate at which velocity changes due to net force
The total distance the object traveled over time
The mass of the object measured in kilograms
The position of the object at a given time

quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Net force causes acceleration: the greater the net force, the greater the acceleration. Acceleration is always in the same direction as the net force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'equilibrium' mean in terms of net force?

options.text: The net force is very large and unbalanced
The object is accelerating in one direction
The net force is zero and forces are balanced
Only gravity is acting on the object here

quiz_questions.explanation: Equilibrium means the net force on an object is zero because all forces are balanced. The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity with no acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'newton' mean as a unit?

options.text: A unit of energy equal to one joule
A unit of mass equal to one kilogram
The SI unit of force (1 kg times 1 m/s squared)
A unit of distance equal to one meter

quiz_questions.explanation: A newton (N) is the SI unit of force. One newton is the force needed to accelerate a 1 kilogram mass at 1 meter per second squared. It is named after Sir Isaac Newton.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two students push a desk: one pushes 40 N to the right and the other pushes 25 N to the right. What is the net force?

options.text: 15 N to the right direction
65 N to the right direction
40 N to the right direction
25 N to the right direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Both forces push in the same direction (right), so they add: 40 N + 25 N = 65 N to the right. The desk accelerates to the right with a combined net force of 65 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 10 kg object has a net force of 50 N acting on it. What is its acceleration?

options.text: 5 m/s squared in the direction of force
500 m/s squared in the direction of force
0.2 m/s squared in the direction of force
50 m/s squared in the direction of force

quiz_questions.explanation: Using F = ma, we solve for a: a = F/m = 50 N / 10 kg = 5 m/s squared. The object accelerates at 5 m/s squared in the direction of the net force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A box is pushed with 100 N to the right. Friction pushes 100 N to the left. What happens to the box?

options.text: It accelerates to the right very quickly
It accelerates to the left very quickly
It does not accelerate because forces are balanced
It begins to spin in a circular motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force = 100 N right - 100 N left = 0 N. The forces are balanced, so the box does not accelerate. It either stays at rest or continues at constant velocity if it was already moving.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5 kg ball is kicked with a net force of 25 N. What is the ball's acceleration?

options.text: 125 m/s squared for the ball
0.2 m/s squared for the ball
5 m/s squared for the ball
30 m/s squared for the ball

quiz_questions.explanation: Using F = ma: a = F/m = 25 N / 5 kg = 5 m/s squared. The ball accelerates at 5 m/s squared in the direction of the kick.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two teams play tug of war. Team A pulls with 500 N to the left, Team B pulls with 450 N to the right. What is the net force?

options.text: 50 N to the left (toward Team A)
50 N to the right (toward Team B)
950 N to the left (toward Team A)
0 N because the forces cancel out

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force = 500 N left - 450 N right = 50 N to the left. Team A pulls harder, so the rope accelerates toward Team A with a net force of 50 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car has an engine force of 3000 N forward and total friction of 1000 N backward. What net force acts on the car?

options.text: 4000 N forward on the car
2000 N forward on the car
3000 N backward on the car
1000 N backward on the car

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force = 3000 N forward - 1000 N backward = 2000 N forward. The engine force exceeds friction, so the car accelerates forward with a net force of 2000 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 2 kg toy car accelerates at 3 m/s squared. What net force is acting on it?

options.text: 1.5 N on the toy car
5 N on the toy car
6 N on the toy car
0.67 N on the toy car

quiz_questions.explanation: Using F = ma: F = 2 kg x 3 m/s squared = 6 N. The net force on the toy car is 6 newtons in the direction of acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object has four forces: 10 N up, 10 N down, 8 N right, and 3 N left. What is the net force?

options.text: 31 N in all directions combined
0 N because all forces cancel fully
5 N to the right direction only
5 N to the left direction only

quiz_questions.explanation: Vertical: 10 N up - 10 N down = 0 N (balanced). Horizontal: 8 N right - 3 N left = 5 N right. The net force is 5 N to the right. The object accelerates to the right.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 60 kg skater pushes off a wall and accelerates at 2 m/s squared. What net force did the wall exert?

options.text: 30 N away from the wall
120 N away from the wall
62 N away from the wall
58 N away from the wall

quiz_questions.explanation: Using F = ma: F = 60 kg x 2 m/s squared = 120 N. The wall exerted a 120 N force on the skater, pushing them away from the wall and causing the acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A parachutist weighing 800 N opens a parachute and air resistance increases to 1200 N. What is the net force and what happens?

options.text: 400 N downward, so the parachutist speeds up
400 N upward, so the parachutist slows down
2000 N downward, so the parachutist speeds up
0 N total, so the parachutist stays at same speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force = 1200 N up (air resistance) - 800 N down (gravity) = 400 N upward. The upward net force decelerates the falling parachutist, causing them to slow down until reaching a new, lower terminal velocity.
